1: Jesus going 'to dine at the house of a ruler of the Pharisees' on the Sabbath situates the healing and teaching that follow within an elite Pharisaic household. Table fellowship with the powerful is itself a Christological act: Christ does not refuse the invitation of those who are 'watching him carefully' (paratereo — the same verb used for hostile surveillance in Luke 6:7; Luke 20:20). His willingness to enter hostile spaces, eat at hostile tables, and teach uncomfortable truths is the pattern of his mission — he goes where he is observed in order to be revealed.
2: The man with dropsy (hydrops — fluid retention) appearing 'before him' may be a deliberate test by the Pharisees, presenting a sympathy-provoking but Sabbath-problematic case. In any case, his presence before Jesus is the occasion for another Sabbath healing that reveals who Jesus is. The man's ailment (swollen from retained fluids) is the opposite of the blessing of the kingdom: where Christ comes, life flows freely, abundance replaces retention, healing replaces withholding. The dropsy-healer is himself the living water who quenches every thirst (John 4:14; Luke 7:37-38).
3: 'Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath, or not?' — Jesus addresses the legal question before healing, exposing the watching lawyers' and Pharisees' heart-position. The silence of the lawyers and Pharisees reveals their internal conflict: they cannot affirm the healing (it would validate Jesus) or deny its permissibility (it would appear heartless). Their silence before Christ's question is the silence of bad faith — they know the answer but will not speak it. Christ acts from love; they calculate from self-protection. His question reveals the difference.
4: 'He took him and healed him and sent him away' — the threefold action (took, healed, sent) is characteristically Lukan: Jesus acts with decisiveness, completeness, and releasing generosity. He does not keep those he heals as demonstrations of his power; he heals them and sends them to their own lives. The healing on the Sabbath is the enacted answer to the legal question: the one who is Lord of the Sabbath (Luke 6:5) fulfills the Sabbath's purpose — rest, restoration, liberation — by healing the man who cannot rest in his waterlogged body.
5: 'Which of you, having a son or an ox that has fallen into a well on a Sabbath day, will not immediately pull him out?' — the argument from emergency: if even Sabbath-restrictions yield to rescue a son or ox from a well, how much more should Sabbath-restrictions yield before the healing of a human being who has been in his 'well' of illness for years. The a fortiori logic (from the lesser to the greater) is Christ's consistent Sabbath-healing argument (Luke 13:15-16). He is not breaking the Sabbath but fulfilling its deepest purpose: liberation from every bondage.
6: 'And they could not reply to these things' — the silence of the lawyers and Pharisees in the face of Christ's Sabbath-rescue argument is not the silence of assent but the silence of defeat without capitulation. They cannot answer because the argument is unanswerable within their own legal framework; they will not agree because agreeing would require accepting Jesus's authority. Their silence is the silence of hardened hearts before the truth — a silence that will deepen into active conspiracy rather than repentance.
7: The parable of the wedding seats is addressed to guests Jesus observed choosing places of honor (tous prōtoklisias — the couches nearest the host, the highest honor in ancient Mediterranean dining). The observation becomes a parable about the kingdom's honor-logic. Christ, the one who sat in the highest place (Philippians 2:6 — 'in the form of God... equal with God') took the lowest place (Philippians 2:7-8 — 'emptied himself... humbled himself to death on a cross'). His parable is his own biography: the humble exalted, the proud brought low.
8: 'When you are invited by someone to a wedding feast, do not sit down in a place of honor, lest someone more distinguished than you be invited by him' — the wisdom of the kingdom is the wisdom of self-lowering. The Proverbs-wisdom background (Proverbs 25:6-7 — 'do not put yourself forward in the king's presence or stand in the place of the great') anticipates what Christ embodies: he who is the King of kings comes not to the highest place but to the manger, the carpenter's shop, the cross. Kingdom wisdom is incarnation-shaped.
9: 'And he who invited you both will come and say to you, Give your place to this person, and then you will begin with shame to take the lowest place' — the public shame of being displaced from a self-claimed seat captures the eschatological dimension of self-exaltation. In the kingdom's final accounting, every self-promoted position is vacated; every self-inflated claim is punctured. The one who pushed to the front will be directed to the back. Christ's passion is the supreme example of this: the one who was truly greatest was pushed to the back — crucified outside the city gates.
10: 'But when you are invited, go and sit in the lowest place, so that when your host comes he may say to you, Friend, move up higher' — the humility-exaltation reversal is not a social strategy for getting higher seats but a description of the kingdom's operating principle. Christ takes the lowest place (incarnation, death, burial) so that the Father says 'Friend, move up higher' (resurrection, ascension, enthronement at the right hand). The host's invitation to move up is the resurrection vindication of the one who died in apparent defeat.
11: 'For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and everyone who humbles himself will be exalted' — this aphorism (also Luke 18:14) is the law of the kingdom derived from Christ's own life-pattern. His descent from the Father's presence through incarnation and crucifixion is the ultimate self-humbling; his resurrection-ascension-enthronement is the ultimate exaltation. Every disciple's cross-bearing participates in this pattern: 'if we have died with him, we will also live with him; if we endure, we will also reign with him' (2 Timothy 2:11-12).
12: 'When you give a dinner or a banquet, do not invite your friends or your brothers or your relatives or rich neighbors, lest they also invite you in return and you be repaid' — the reciprocity of conventional hospitality (invite those who will invite you back) is the social logic that Christ transcends in his own table-fellowship. He eats with those who cannot repay: tax collectors, sinners, the sick, the marginal. His table-fellowship is not calculated reciprocity but gratuitous grace — the form of the kingdom's invitation which he himself embodies and now teaches.
13: 'But when you give a feast, invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, the blind' — the four categories (poor, crippled, lame, blind) are the same groups who appear in the next parable as those invited to the great banquet (Luke 14:21) and in the Jubilee proclamation of Luke 4:18 (poor, captive, blind, oppressed). Christ himself declares his own invitation to these groups (Luke 7:22 — 'the blind receive their sight, the lame walk, lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, the poor have good news preached to them'). The teaching reflects his own practice.
14: 'And you will be blessed, because they cannot repay you. For you will be repaid at the resurrection of the just' — the eschatological repayment (at the resurrection) makes kingdom hospitality a form of investment in the age to come rather than social capital in this age. Christ's own death is the supreme example: he gave everything for those who could not repay him; his repayment is the resurrection and the gathering of those he redeemed. 'The resurrection of the just' is the moment when every act of unrequited kingdom-love receives its divine recompense.
15: 'When one of those who reclined at table with him heard these things, he said to him, Blessed is everyone who will eat bread in the kingdom of God!' — the anonymous table-companion's exclamation about the eschatological banquet triggers the parable of the Great Banquet. His beatitude is correct but complacent: he assumes his own place at the kingdom table. The parable will challenge this assumption. Christ himself is the host of the great banquet (Revelation 19:7-9 — the wedding supper of the Lamb) whose invitations are being sent now and whose table includes many the invited guests refused to attend.
16: 'A man once gave a great banquet and invited many' — the great banquet of the kingdom (Isaiah 25:6 — 'a feast of rich food for all peoples, a feast of well-aged wine, of rich food full of marrow, of aged wine well refined') is prepared by the host who is Christ himself. His incarnation is the preparation of the banquet; his cross is the slaughter of the fatted animals; his resurrection and mission are the sending of the servants. The 'many' who are invited are Israel first — the covenant people first invited to the messianic feast.
17: 'And at the time for the banquet he sent his servant to say to those who had been invited, Come, for everything is now ready' — the servant's announcement 'everything is now ready' is the gospel proclamation: the cross has accomplished what is needed; the invitation stands. Christ sends his servants (the apostles) to those already invited (Israel) saying 'come now.' The 'now' of the ready banquet is the eschatological now of the new covenant: the time for preparation is past; the feast is ready; the invitation is urgent.
18: 'But they all alike began to make excuses' — the unanimous refusal of the original invitees (who had accepted the invitation in advance) is the parable's indictment of Israel's rejection of Christ. The three excuses (land, oxen, wife) represent legitimate activities that have been elevated above the urgent invitation. None of the excuses is sinful in itself; all of them are disqualifying when they replace the kingdom's invitation. The tragedy is not wickedness but priority-inversion: good things treated as ultimate things crowd out the truly ultimate.
19: 'I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I go to examine them' — the agricultural investment excuse represents the distraction of legitimate business from the kingdom's invitation. The oxen-purchaser is not evil; he is reasonable by worldly standards. But the kingdom's invitation is not a reasonable option to balance against other reasonable options: it is the summons of the Lord himself to the eschatological feast. The reasonable man who misses the feast for a reasonable reason misses it just as completely as the unreasonable man who refuses with contempt.
20: 'I have married a wife, and therefore I cannot come' — the marital excuse is the most poignant: the institution God established (marriage, Genesis 2:24) becomes the reason for refusing God's invitation. There is a discipleship-cost here: even the most intimate and legitimate human bonds must not override the kingdom's call. Jesus himself said that love for him must outweigh even family love (Luke 14:26 — hating father, mother, wife in comparison). The married man's excuse represents the good things of this life that can functionally replace God as the ultimate priority.
21: 'Go out quickly to the streets and lanes of the city, and bring in the poor and crippled and blind and lame' — the Jubilee-groups (again: poor, crippled, blind, lame — from Luke 4:18; Luke 14:13) are invited when the originally-invited refuse. This is the pattern of Christ's own ministry: when the religious establishment rejected him, his table opened to sinners, tax collectors, the marginal, the unclean. The banquet is filled — but with those the Pharisee-table-companion (Luke 14:15) would have considered unworthy. The host's generosity overflows every social boundary.
22: 'And the servant said, Sir, what you commanded has been done, and still there is room' — the abundance of the banquet (there is still room after all the poor and marginalized have been invited) is the eschatological superabundance of the kingdom. Grace is not a limited resource that runs out when the 'deserving' have received it; it overflows every vessel. The 'still there is room' is the gospel's inexhaustible invitation: Christ's table has never been full, the welcoming arms of the father (Luke 15:20) are never withdrawn, the door is open until the hour of closing (Luke 13:25).
23: 'Go out to the highways and hedges and compel people to come in, that my house may be filled' — the second expansion of the invitation (from city-poor to country-outsiders) represents the Gentile mission. The compulsion (anankason — compel) is not coercion but urgent persuasion: the host's insistence that his table be full drives the servants to seek those who would never self-invite. Augustine famously used this verse to justify state-compelled religion — a gross misapplication. The compulsion is the persuasion of the gospel: 'we implore you on behalf of Christ, be reconciled to God' (2 Corinthians 5:20).
24: 'For I tell you, none of those men who were invited shall taste my banquet' — the solemn exclusion of the original-invitees-who-refused is the judgment word at the parable's end. They will not taste what they declined. The great banquet is not perpetually open: there is a moment of closure (the banquet begins, the door shuts, Luke 13:25). Those who persistently refuse the invitation — whether through explicit rejection or through good-excuse priority-inversion — are excluded by their own choices. The tragedy is that the feast was prepared for them; they simply would not come.
25: 'Now great crowds accompanied him' — the large crowd following Jesus on the road to Jerusalem sets the scene for the hardest discipleship-cost teachings. The popularity of Jesus's following does not soften his demands; it sharpens them. He turns to address the crowds (Luke 14:25 — 'he turned and said to them') with the most demanding words he will speak about discipleship. The crowd's enthusiasm is precisely the moment Christ calls for honest cost-counting: following him to the cross requires more than crowd-momentum.
26: 'If anyone comes to me and does not hate his own father and mother and wife and children and brothers and sisters, yes, and even his own life, he cannot be my disciple' — 'hate' (miseo) here is the Semitic comparative idiom for 'love less than': love for Christ must relativize every other love. This is the demand of the First Commandment (no other gods before me) applied to discipleship. Christ himself models this: he left the Father's presence (John 17:5 — the glory he had before the world) for the Incarnation; he submitted his natural desire to live to the Father's will at Gethsemane (Luke 22:42). Absolute love for God relativizes all other loves.
27: 'Whoever does not bear his own cross and come after me cannot be my disciple' — the cross-bearing saying (also 9:23 with 'daily') places the instrument of Christ's death as the identifying mark of his followers. Before the cross was the supreme sacrifice-altar, it was a Roman execution-device — humiliation, public shaming, slow death. The disciples are called into the posture of the condemned: accepting social death, embracing shame for Christ's sake, following him to the place of execution. The one who bore the cross first (Luke 23:26) calls all who follow him to take up their own.
28: 'For which of you, desiring to build a tower, does not first sit down and count the cost?' — the tower-builder parable applies practical wisdom to discipleship. The wisdom of honest cost-assessment before undertaking a project is universally recognized; Christ applies it to the decision to follow him. The 'cost' of discipleship is the entire self (Luke 14:26 — his own life, Luke 14:27 — his own cross). Those who begin without counting the cost will abandon the project when the price becomes clear. Honest disciples are those who have reckoned with the full cost before beginning.
29: 'Otherwise, when he has laid a foundation and is not able to finish, all who see it begin to mock him' — the public mockery of the half-completed tower is the eschatological failure of half-hearted discipleship. Those who begin the way of the cross and then abandon it are exposed before the watching world — and before the eschatological judgment — as having miscalculated. The shame of abandonment is greater than the shame of never starting. Christ's own completion of the cross (John 19:30 — 'It is finished') is the model of the fully-counted, fully-paid, fully-completed cost.
30: 'This man began to build and was not able to finish' — the mocking of the failed builder is the counterpoint to the completed tower. The failed builder represents the disciple who began with enthusiasm but without genuine cost-counting — the shallow soil (Luke 8:13 — 'in time of testing fall away'). Christ's call to discipleship is not a call to begin with enthusiasm; it is a call to continue to the end. The one who endures to the end will be saved (Matthew 10:22); the one who falls away in the middle has demonstrated that the foundation was insufficient.
31: 'Or what king, going to encounter another king in war, will not sit down first and deliberate whether he is able with ten thousand to meet him who comes against him with twenty thousand?' — the military cost-counting parable applies the same principle to the assessment of one's own resources against the enemy's. The disciple must honestly reckon: am I strong enough to follow Christ all the way to the cross? The honest answer is: no, not in my own strength. The parable implicitly points to the necessity of Christ's resources supplementing the disciple's own — discipleship is not self-reliance but union with the one who has already defeated every enemy.
32: 'And if not, while the other is yet a great way off, he sends a delegation and asks for terms of peace' — the king who cannot match the opposing force seeks terms of peace before the battle. Applied to discipleship: the one who honestly reckons his inability to follow Christ must seek terms of peace with the one demanding unconditional surrender. Christ is the opposing king who comes with overwhelmingly superior force — the only rational response is surrender, which is discipleship. The 'terms of peace' are the terms of the kingdom: lose your life, take up the cross, follow me.
33: 'So therefore, any one of you who does not renounce all that he has cannot be my disciple' — the conclusion of both cost-counting parables: discipleship requires the renunciation of all possessions (apotassetai pasin tois heautou hyparchousin). This is the radical version of the kingdom's economics: everything the disciple holds must be held loosely, as a steward not an owner. Christ himself renounced all: 'though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich' (2 Corinthians 8:9). His total renunciation enables and models total discipleship.
34: 'Salt is good, but if salt has lost its taste, how shall its saltiness be restored?' — the salt metaphor for discipleship follows the renunciation-teaching: disciples who have abandoned the cross-shaped life are like salt that has lost its saltiness — outwardly similar but without the essential quality that makes them useful. The Sermon on the Mount calls disciples 'the salt of the earth' (Matthew 5:13). Christ is himself the salt — the preserving, flavoring, purifying presence that disciples carry. Without the cross-shaped life, discipleship is the form without the substance.
35: 'It is of no use either for the soil or for the manure pile. It is thrown away. He who has ears to hear, let him hear' — the uselessness of saltless salt (it cannot even fertilize!) is the uselessness of cross-less discipleship. The summons to 'hear' (akouetō — the same prophetic summons as 8:8 and Luke 14:35) closes the discipleship-cost section. The ears needed to hear this teaching are the ears opened by the Spirit (Acts 2:37 — 'cut to the heart'). Without the Spirit's illumination, these demands appear harsh; with it, they appear liberating — the path of the cross is the path of resurrection.
